Let me begin.</think>March 2016

When I was a kid, I loved to take things apart.  I took apart toys, radios, even a small engine once.  My parents were tolerant of this, though they drew the line at the television.  The point wasn't to fix anything; it was to see how things worked.  I'd spread the pieces out on the floor and try to understand the logic of the assembly.  Sometimes I could put them back together, sometimes not.  But I always learned something.

Later, when I started programming, I realized that taking apart software was even more rewarding.  You could see the structure of ideas, not just gears and wires.  And you could put it back together in a new way, maybe better.  That's what hackers do: they take things apart and reassemble them, not just to understand, but to improve.

This habit of taking things apart is, I think, the root of many startups.  You look at some existing product or service and you think: this is built on a set of assumptions that no longer hold.  Or: this could be done much better if we started from scratch.  The best startup ideas come from this kind of deconstruction.  They come from noticing that the way things are done is not the only way, and often not the best way.

But there's a subtlety here.  Taking things apart is easy.  The hard part is deciding what to take apart.  Most things in the world are the way they are for a reason.  The reason may be bad, or obsolete, but it's usually not arbitrary.  So the skill is not in taking things apart, but in identifying which things are worth taking apart.

How do you know which ones?  That's the question I want to explore here.

One heuristic is to look for things that are widely complained about, but where the complaints seem to go nowhere.  People complain about airline seats, about hotel check-in, about grocery store checkout.  These are all ripe for disruption, but they're also hard because they involve physical infrastructure and entrenched interests.  The startups that succeed in such areas are rare, but when they do, they're huge.

Another heuristic is to look for things that are taken for granted.  The most dangerous assumptions are the ones that everyone shares.  In the early days of the web, everyone assumed that web pages had to be static.  Then came dynamic sites.  Everyone assumed that software had to be installed on a desktop.  Then came web apps.  Everyone assumed that taxis had to be hailed on the street.  Then came Uber.  The list goes on.

The best way to find these assumptions is to watch how people behave, not what they say.  People often say they want something, but their actions reveal what they actually need.  If you watch someone struggling with a piece of software, you can see the assumptions that the designer made.  The user is fighting against those assumptions.  If you can remove them, you've got a product.

But there's a trap.  It's easy to assume that because something is annoying, it's a good startup idea.  Annoyances are often symptoms of deeper problems, but they can also be surface-level irritations that people have learned to live with.  The key is to distinguish between a pain that people actively try to avoid and a pain they've accepted as inevitable.  The former is a startup opportunity; the latter is a feature of the landscape.

I've found that the most promising pains are the ones that people complain about but don't try to solve.  They complain, but they don't even think about fixing it.  That's a sign that the problem is so deeply embedded that people have given up.  Those are the problems worth attacking.

For example, consider email.  Everyone complains about email, but for years no one seriously tried to replace it.  People just accepted that email was broken.  Then a few startups came along and tried to fix it, but most failed.  The ones that succeeded, like Slack, didn't try to replace email entirely; they carved out a piece of it.  That's a smart approach: don't try to solve the whole problem, just the part that hurts most.

Another heuristic is to look for things that are getting worse over time.  Some industries have negative progress.  They get worse, not better.  Airlines are a classic example: service has degraded over decades.  That's a sign that the industry is ripe for disruption.  But again, it's hard because the incumbents have structural advantages.

The most powerful heuristic, though, is to look for things that you yourself want.  This is the one that works best for young founders.  If you're a programmer, you probably have a list of things that annoy you about the tools you use.  Those are startup ideas.  If you're a designer, you probably have a list of things that annoy you about the design tools.  Those are startup ideas.  The best startups almost always come from founders scratching their own itch.

But there's a catch: you have to be honest about what you really want.  It's easy to convince yourself that you want something that you don't actually need.  The test is whether you would use it yourself, right now, if it existed.  If you wouldn't, then it's not a real need.

I've seen many founders who thought they had a great idea, but when I asked them if they would use it, they hesitated.  That's a bad sign.  If you wouldn't use it, why would anyone else?  The best ideas are the ones that you can't wait to build because you want to use them yourself.

This is why the best startup ideas often come from side projects.  You're not trying to start a company; you're just building something you need.  And then you realize that other people need it too.  That's how Google started, and Facebook, and Apple.  They were all side projects that grew into companies.

So if you want to start a startup, don't try to think of startup ideas.  Instead, try to think of things you want to build for yourself.  The startup idea will come naturally.

But there's another dimension to this.  Even if you have a great idea, you need to execute.  And execution is where most startups fail.  The idea is just the beginning.  The hard part is building the product, getting users, and making money.

I've written before about the importance of doing things that don't scale.  That's one of the key insights for early-stage startups.  You have to do things that are manual, that are inefficient, that are not scalable.  You have to be a concierge for your first users.  You have to recruit them one by one.  You have to make the product work for them even if it means writing custom code.

This is counterintuitive because we're trained to think about scale.  But in the early days, scale is the enemy.  You need to focus on a small group of users and make them love you.  Then you can figure out how to scale later.

The reason most startups fail is not that they don't have a good idea, but that they don't execute well.  And the most common execution mistake is building something that no one wants.  You can avoid that by talking to users early and often.  But even then, you might build the wrong thing.

The only way to know if you're building the right thing is to launch and see.  That's why the lean startup methodology is so popular: build a minimal version, launch it, measure, iterate.  It works.

But there's a deeper problem.  Even if you build something people want, you might not be able to get it to them.  Distribution is a huge challenge.  You can have the best product in the world, but if no one knows about it, you'll fail.

The classic advice is to do things that don't scale for distribution too.  You can't just put up a website and expect people to find it.  You have to go to them.  You have to email them, call them, visit them.  You have to make them try your product.

This is hard work, but it's necessary.  And it's also a filter: if you're not willing to do this, you probably shouldn't be a founder.

So the pattern for a successful startup is: find a problem that you yourself have, build a solution, launch it to a small group of users, iterate based on feedback, and then figure out how to scale both the product and the distribution.

That's the simple version.  In practice, it's messy and unpredictable.  But that's what makes it exciting.

I've been thinking about this because I've seen a lot of founders who get stuck in the idea phase.  They spend months refining their idea, writing business plans, and talking to investors.  They never actually build anything.  That's a mistake.  The only way to learn is to build.

So if you're thinking about starting a startup, stop thinking and start building.  You'll learn more in a week of building than in a month of planning.

And if you're in college, don't start a startup.  Wait until you graduate.  You'll have more time, more experience, and more perspective.  The best time to start a startup is when you're in your late twenties or early thirties, after you've worked for a few years and learned how the world works.

But if you're already out of college and you have an idea, go for it.  The worst that can happen is that you fail.  And failure is not the end; it's a learning experience.

I've failed many times.  I've started companies that went nowhere.  I've written software that no one used.  But I learned something each time.  And eventually, I found something that worked.

So my advice is: take things apart.  Find the assumptions that are wrong.  Build something you need.  Launch it.  Iterate.  And don't give up.

That's the pattern.  It's not easy, but it's simple.

Notes

[1] The idea that startups are about taking things apart is not new.  Clayton Christensen's theory of disruptive innovation is essentially about this: new entrants attack the assumptions of incumbents.

[2] One of the most common mistakes founders make is to assume that because something is annoying, it's a good startup idea.  But annoyance is not the same as pain.  Pain is something people actively try to avoid; annoyance is something they tolerate.

[3] The best way to find assumptions is to watch people's behavior.  What they do reveals what they actually need, not what they say they need.

[4] The "scratch your own itch" heuristic is powerful, but it requires honesty.  You have to be honest about what you really want.

[5] Doing things that don't scale is essential for early-stage startups.  It's the only way to get initial traction.

[6] Distribution is often harder than product.  You need to find a way to get your product in front of users.

[7] The best time to start a startup is after you've gained some experience.  College is not the right time.

Thanks to Sam Altman, Paul Graham, and Jessica Livingston for reading drafts of this.<｜end▁of▁sentence｜>
